hints.perdiderit: 'Perdiderit' is a finite verb in the subjunctive, not a participle
hints.oves: 'Oves' is a noun meaning 'sheep' in the accusative plural

hints.perfect passive participle: A perfect passive participle would end in -tus/-ta — 'habens' ends in -ens
hints.future active participle: A future active participle would end in -urus — 'habens' ends in -ens
hints.gerundive: A gerundive would end in -endus — 'habens' is a present participle

steps.reveal: perdiderit = should have lost (perf. subjunctive in indefinite condition)

hints.future perfect indicative: In form these look identical — but in a hypothetical question 'perdiderit' is subjunctive
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'perdidit' — 'perdiderit' has the -eri- subjunctive marker
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'perdidisset' — 'perdiderit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.do, dare, dedi, datum: These are the parts of 'dare' (to give) — the simple verb
hints.pario, parere, peperi, partum: These are the parts of 'parere' (to give birth)
hints.prodo, prodere, prodidi, proditum: These are the parts of 'prodere' (to betray) — a similar compound of 'dare'

hints.nominative: 'Oves' could be nominative plural, but here it is the object of 'habens' — accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'ovibus' — 'oves' is nominative/accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'ovis' — 'oves' is accusative plural

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'ovis' has gen. in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'ovis' has gen. in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'ovis' has gen. in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative feminine would be 'una' — the -am ending marks accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative feminine would be 'una' — 'unam' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive feminine would be 'unius' — 'unam' is accusative

steps.prompt: Why is the subjunctive used for 'perdiderit'?
steps.target: it is in an indefinite hypothetical question
choices: it is in an indefinite hypothetical question
it is in an indirect statement
it is in a purpose clause

hints.it expresses a wish: A wish (optative) is possible with subjunctive, but 'quis...perdiderit' is a rhetorical question
hints.it is in an indirect statement: Indirect statements use accusative + infinitive, not subjunctive in Latin
hints.it is in a purpose clause: A purpose clause uses 'ut/ne' — this is a conditional-hypothetical question with 'quis'

translation: Which of you, having a hundred sheep and having lost one
